Geologists found a mineral from the subsoil of another planet on Earth 2.09.2019, 13: 38 1,312 It was discovered in southeastern Australia.
Back in 1951, a meteorite was discovered in the province of Victoria, in the extreme south-east of Australia, which is now in the local museum.
So far, scientists carefully examine a 220-gram sample, from which only 71 grams remained today.
The Victoria Museum carefully controls the "consumption" of rare meteorite substances and only in 2018 approved the transfer of a small amount to study the Californian mineralogs Chi Ma (Chi Ma) and Alan Rubin (Alan Rubin).
The results of this work were presented in an article published in the journal American Mineralogist, writes naked-science.ru.
According to them, in the depth of the sample, it was possible to detect and describe a previously unknown mineral, which was called edscottite, in honor of the famous meteorite and "space" chemistry researcher Edward Scott, working at the University of Hawaii.
Scientists believe that the mineral was formed by the interaction of iron and carbon atoms during the slow cooling of the meteorite.
Such structures are not formed for a long time in the smelting of steel, but they can only be called a complete mineral now that such a sample has been discovered in natural conditions.
The authors suggest that the unique meteorite is a fragment of the planetesimal, which appeared in the age of the youth of the Solar System, but has not been able to become a full-fledged planet.
Edscottitis could have been formed in it under the influence of high temperatures caused by the radioactivity of heavy elements.
The same was once our Earth, but this planet was not destined to grow.
The accidental cosmic catastrophe — most likely a collision with another massive body — destroyed the embryo of the future world, and the debris from it scattered through the Solar System.
Most of them, apparently, were absorbed by planets and satellites, burned on the Sun, but a lot of them remained in the asteroid belt.
And at least one came to us, being in the Australian Museum.

Thousands of years ago, medicine was not an exact science, and that was the case in all parts of the world.
To put it straight, the practitioners experimented, but if we look for more - less cultural words for this, we can say that all creative treatments appeared just then.
Ancient Egyptians, for example, prescribed exotic medicines such as the blood of a lizard, dead mice, dirt and moldy bread.
They sincerely believed that horse saliva helped women with libido disorders, and the Romans, in order to treat their teeth, regularly cleaned them with urine, which is rich in ammonia.
However, one of the strangest treatments described in medical ancient books — if it was actually considered as a treatment — is skull trepanation, common among the Incas, when five or more holes were made surgically in the skull while the patients were still alive. Why?
No one still knows that.
The first Inca skulls with more than five holes were found more than a hundred years ago by members of the same expedition that opened the lost city of Machu Picchu to the world.
After plugging in a Peruvian burial cave, archaeologists stumbled upon a pair of these skulls, filled with surgically made holes with perfectly round edges and shapes of the same size.
Often these holes were tightened from above by new layers of bone tissue The fact that some of the people who were made such "operations", they survived, suggests that the drilling of such holes had in its basis medical purposes, akin to trepanation - the second oldest surgical procedure after circumcision. Although the practice of making holes in the skull dates back to the stone age, the Greek father of medicine Hippocrates entered the the number of "authoritative doctors" who supported this practice, as he reflected in his book On Head Injuries.
The tool, called trepan, was used to make a hole in the skull and remove part of the cranial bone, exposing the open brain.
After the wound healed, the surviving patient remained with a dent on his skull for the rest of his life.
As a rule, in the ancient and Middle Ages trepanation was carried out to patients who were considered obsessed with evil spirits.
It was also believed that with such a hole in the skull, the brain was relieved of excessive pressure.
Some scientists, however, argue that sometimes doctors did trepanation in cases that modern medicine would also consider to be the focus of current neurosurgery — treatment of migraines, epilepsy, hydrocephalus and mental disorders.
It is surprising, however, that trepanation still exists today, being adopted by pseudo-scientific currents.
They emerged from a movement that began in the 1960s and was founded by Dane Bart Hughes.
Amazing, however, were the times.
Practicing doctors even had their own organization, the International Group of Trepanation Defenders, whose members claimed on their website that a hole made in the skull helped to restore pressure in the vessels of the brain.
Arteries and capillaries can expand again, filling with large amounts of blood and forcing out about the same volume of cerebrospinal fluid, which with age circulates worse and worse.
The consequence of this should be the return of a significant amount of energy that has been lost in the process of growing up and aging the body.
Amanda Fielding is going to a party an hour after her trepanation has been done. On vice.com, there is an article in which a woman named Amanda Fielding gives instipation, many years after she has expressed her desire to trepanate herself to expand her consciousness (watch carefully – shots are not for the fainted).
In 1970, when she was 27 years old, she made a film about herself, capturing an act of vandalism and opening her own skull.
Some of the rock stars of the time, who managed to see the film, began to actively discuss it, so it became a sort of cult sensation.
"I am incredibly cautious, even paranoid, full of fear of a man... but I am a sculptor and I can quite make a hole in my head.
I used a flat-bottomed drill with a foot drive for this, "Fielding said in an interview.
“The problem is that trepanation only sounds wild, but there is a serious hypothesis explaining how it improves cerebral circulation, and I researched this hypothesis with leading Russian neurologists. ”
(Of these Amanda mentions the name only Yuri Moskalenko).
So, if trepanation really seems ridiculous now, then of course we should appreciate the efforts of the ancient doctors for trying to explore this direction.
Especially in the Inca Empire, where it was easy to crack the skull, it was a very common thing.
John Verano, a forensic anthropologist and chief expert who has studied holes in Inca turtles for more than twenty years, says that violence has always been widespread in Latin America.
He personally analyzed more than 800 skulls with at least one hole in them and concluded that most of them were obtained from a slab of dust or oak.
About 2,400 years ago, at about the same time as Hippocrates, only at the other end of the world, Inca doctors and shamans began to treat these chopped heads, essentially making another hole in the skull.
They probably did it to cleanse the wound, drain the resulting clot, or give the brain a better diet.
On the basis of a study of skulls, where such holes are overgrown with a new layer of bone tissue, Verano concluded that the survival rate after such operations was about 40%.
By the time the Spaniards appeared on the Inca horizon, survival had jumped to 80%, which gives us reason to state that the Inca had a good neurosurgery by then.
Personally, it seems to me that these figures are not quite real, given that the survival rate after trepanation in the 1800s rarely exceeded 10% in the best hospitals in the world in those days.
Maybe he had a sample of the incredibly lucky owners of these skulls?
As soon as the Spaniards conquered the Incas, the skulls with mysterious holes ceased to appear.
However, their mystery remains.
“I doubt that one person would be injured five times with a small object, and every time the wound was cleared of bone fragments, and every time the person would remain alive, ” said Verano in an interview with CNN,“ meaning that such examples, when people were drilled a hole in the skull over and over again, could happen on different grounds and have a different background.
This may be, as in other cases of trepanation, the desire to make individuals more conscious, intelligent, or, as in the case of Amanda Fielding, to contribute to the expansion of their consciousness through increased blood flow to the brain.
Verano, however, did not find any evidence for this idea, so he does not support it.
Instead, he suggests that trepanation was done to ease migraine attacks.
As noted earlier, head injuries were commonplace, and the victim of such injuries could suffer from severe headaches all her life because of a concussion.
The reasons why the Inca doctors could think that it would be right to drill several holes in this situation can be lost forever: we may never recognize them, says Verano.

On Friday, July 9, the new press secretary of Zelensky, Sergey Nikiforov, answering the question "Countries" at his first briefing, said that the Office of the President will not respond to the publication of memoirs by the former press secretary of Zelensky Julia Mendel, where, according to the first announcements, the internal intrigues of the Office of the President will be covered.
"Just today I saw an interview with Julia, where she confessed that there are no scandals to expect.
Because she respects her commitment to the people she worked with.
So there will be no serious scandals, no insiders, no secrets.
So I don't think we have to comment on anything.
After all, let's wait for the book to come out and see what it says.
If this will concern some fact, then we will already come out with a reaction to you, "Nikiforov said.
He added that he was not going to read the book of his predecessor, Julia Mendel.
"I don't think I'm going to read.
I have a little time now due to the fact that I need to grasp a huge amount of information, so there will definitely be no time for Ms. Julia's book, "said Sergey Nikiforov.
The "Country" reported that shortly after the dismissal of the president's press secretary, Mendel wrote a book of memoirs "Each of us is the President" about her two-year work at the Office of the President.
In the author 's book Mendel will talk about previously unknown facts, insiders and events that accompanied her during her work as a press secretary of the head of state.
In the sale of memoirs will appear on July 11, the cost of the book is 190 hryvnia.
We took a look at the passage of Mendel's book in our material.
In it, the author reflects on the lessons of history for the people, identity and languages.
A Soviet soldier who defeated Nazism, Mendel sees himself as an unbalanced rapist with alcohol addiction.
Russian-speaking Ukrainians, in its understanding, certainly miss the USSR and "drown" for Russia.
Recall that Zelensky today signed decrees on the dismissal of Mendel and the appointment of a new press secretary.
They became the TV presenter of the TV channel "Ukraine 24" Sergey Nikiforov.

Scientists have discovered in one of the meteorites from the Moon a new mineral - Donwilhelmsit.
They report the discovery and its significance for "processes occurring in the subsoil of the Earth" in American Mineralogist.
A team of scientists led by Jörg Fritz of the Center for Research in German Nerdlingen discovered a new mineral in the moon meteorite Oued Awlitis 001, which was accidentally found by local residents in Western Sahara in 2014.
Geologists then determined that this meteorite, weighing about 380 grams, once landed from the Moon in space during an asteroid attack, and then reached Earth.
Analysis of the process of three-dimensional scattering of electrons showed that it consists mainly of calcium silicate, aluminum (CaAl 4 Si 2 O 11), silicon and oxygen with hexagonal crystalline structure.
And it resembles the structure of materials of the same composition, which are formed in experimental conditions at extreme temperatures and pressures.
"I've been working on meteorites every day for 25 years, but to be the first to discover a new mineral from space, and then start exploring it, a terrific feeling," says the co-author of the study, Ansgar Greshak.
The name Donwilhelmsite (Donwilhelmsite) received a new mineral in honor of the American moon explorer Don Wilhelms.
The recently discovered Donwilhelmsite also occurs on Earth - in the depths of the earthly mantle: "Donwilhelmsite is formed in the Earth's mantle during the deep processing of alumina materials of the earth's crust containing aluminum," German scientists say. "This is probably the main carrier of aluminum and calcium from deposits in most of the transition zone and in the upper part of the lower mantle of the Earth (460-700 km)."
"If the Donwilhelmsite is then transported back up through mantle plumes, at a depth of about 460 kilometers it disintegrates into other minerals, such as pomegranate, kianite, or clinopyroxen," researchers write.
According to scientists, more than 5,200 minerals are officially discovered, some of which are so rare that they can be found only in one place on Earth.
But experts estimate that at least 1,500 other minerals are still awaiting discovery.
Among them are exotic minerals from the subsoil of the Earth or even extraterrestrial origin.
"The discovery of the Donwilhelmsite mineral is of great importance for the study of the history of the formation of the Moon and the processes occurring in the subsoil of the Earth," scientists say.

This town was discovered 110 years ago by American archaeologist Heirem...
RIA Novosti, 24.07.2021 2021-07-24T08: 00 2021-07-24T08: 00 2021-07-24T08: 02 Religion and worldview Yale University Chrystofor Columbus Nicaragua Peru Mexica / html / head / meta [@ name = 'og: title'] / @ content / html / head / meta [@ name = 'og: description version'] / @ content https: / / cdn22.img.ria.ru / images / 99949 / 04 / 999490448 _ 0: 104: 800: 554 _ 1920x0 _ 80 _ 0 _ 0 _ 925358868682cec7f7941d61e4005636.jpg MOSCOW, 24 Jul — RIA Novosti, Nikita Bizin.
Millions of tourists come to Peru to see the ancient Machu Picchu, lost high in the mountains.
This town was discovered 110 years ago by American archaeologist Heirem Bingham.
At first, he decided that he had found the royal residence.
But, as further research has shown, things are much more interesting.
About the greatest creation of the Incas - in the material of RIA Novosti. The city in the cloudsThe New World as if deliberately presented to invading strangers surprises.
For example, Christopher Columbus sought an alternative route to India, but discovered a whole continent.
And the associate professor of Yale University, Heirem Bingham, dreamed of finding Vilcabamba, a legendary city where in the 16th century the Incas allegedly took all their treasures, hiding them from the Spaniards.
On July 24, 1911, an American explorer wandered the mountains accompanied by a local peasant boy and a sergeant of the Peruvian army, trying to find anything valuable.
And his hopes came true. "Hardly did we leave the cabin and surround the protrusion of the mountain, as we saw a striking view: the majestic sight of the extended terraces...
Suddenly, I found stone walls in front of me, the ruins of houses.
It was not easy to see them, because they were partially overgrown with trees and moss — the work of several centuries — but in a dense shade, hiding in the overgrowth of bamboo and tangled vines, there and there were painted walls of white granite, the stones were carefully cut and with great skill adjusted to each other, "Bingham wrote. The city he called Machu. - Picchu - translated from the Quechua language, it means" old mountain. "
The scientist then thought he had found the cherished Wilkabambu.Bingham version The Bingham team began excavations.
There were several types of buildings in the city: the royal palace, sanctuaries and commoners' houses.
The scientist decided that the residence of the supreme ruler of the Inca Empire was located here.
But one shocking finding made him doubt it. Not far from the city found a mass grave - 173 skeletons.
An osteologist from the archaeological group, George Eaton, has determined that almost all of them belong to women.
And Bingham was sure: these are the remains of the virgins of the sun - priestess of the deity Inti and personal servants of the ruler. However, after a hundred years, researchers proved the error of archaeologists - in Machu Picchu not only female bones.
About half of them were male.
Moreover, Professor of the Department of Anthropology of the University of Tulane John Verano found out: buried there people killed. It turns out, Bingham was mistaken - and Machu Picchu is not the tsarist city of Vilkabamba.
The question is, what is it in the case. Shocking detailsAnthropologist John Verano told about the important detail: on all skeletons there are traces of knife strokes.
His arguments were confirmed during the repeated excavations in Machu Picchu - archaeologists found a ritual T-shaped knife. This circumstance led the specialists to a dead end.
After all, the religious rites of the Incas, unlike their neighbors the Aztecs and the Maya, did not imply a chilling cruelty — they did not cut out human hearts or sacrifice to the gods of infants.
The higher forces were mainly served by plants: corn, coca leaves, vegetables.
The most priests could do was carcass the lamas.
Or did the Incas also kill people? Bloody ritualsSeveral years ago, two more mass incas were discovered in northern Peru.
In the first - about 30 people.
All the remains had traces of violent death. "Most of them belong to girls aged 15.
The victims were made 600 years ago, just a few decades before the appearance of Spanish conquerors on the continent, "said the archaeologists of the journalist. In the second mound there were 140 more skeletons - and the find shocked scientists." I did not expect this.
Ribs are displaced in children, traces of cuts are visible on the bones.
Supposedly, they were cut out their hearts ", - said in an interview associate professor of the Department of Anthropology of the University of Florida Gabriel Prieto.According to him, as a rule, the hearts of people and animals were presented to the sun, which was worshiped by all civilizations of Mesoamerica (historical and cultural region extending from the center of Mexico to Honduras and Nicaragua). So, the Inca still sacrificed people.
If so, Machu Picchu could have been a place for bloody rituals.
This version is also supported by the features of architecture.
There are three sanctuaries, one of them - the Temple of the Three Windows - in honor of the solar deity.
In addition, on the top of the city stands the ritual construction of Intihuatan.
Quechua is "the place to which the sun is attached". However, experts are not sure that young men and girls whose skeletons were found in the famous city of the Incas were killed during the ritual of carving hearts.
Scientists have another version. Following the emperor, the Tip was found in the testimony of Catholic missionaries who arrived on the continent with the Spanish conquerors.
The preachers tried to expose all, in their opinion, the unenlightened darkness of Indian beliefs.
But at the same time left detailed descriptions of the culture and life of the peoples of Mesoamerica.
Among them there is an interesting detail: "When the Incas had a noble nobleman dying, his servants could be killed so that they could accompany the master in the afterlife." "The Emperor was a sacred figure.
It was a great honor to die for him, "says Peruvian archaeologist Guillermo Coc. There is a possibility that something like this happened in Machu Picchu.
It is known that with the beginning of the Spanish conquest, the last ruler of the Incas withdrew high into the mountains.
I don't know where it is.
Perhaps his last refuge was Machu Picchu.
And a hundred and a half skeletons belong to those who followed the ruler.
That is, it can be a mass suicide, one of the bloodiest in human history. Anyway, the city of the Incas still keeps its secret.
The restrictions of the Peruvian authorities on archeological work in Machu Picchu also prevent it from being discovered.
Officials seem to be reluctant to remove the cover of mystery from "the city in the clouds."

The meteorite found by a group of scientists in the Sahara is likely to belong to a long-lost infant protoplanet.
Scientists discover many amazing meteorites scattered on the surface of the Earth.
A group of scientists led by Jean-Alix Barrat, a geochemist at the University of Western Brittany in France, announced in their study that one of these stones seems to be incredibly ancient, it appeared before the Earth.
They believe it comes from a long-vanished protoplanet, a baby planet that, so to speak, had no chance of growing up.
The name of the meteorite found is EC 002, and it can give a new idea of the first days of the existence of our Solar System.
Erg Czech 002, or EC 002, was discovered in Adrar, Algeria, last May in the near-desert region of Erg-Cech in the Sahara desert.
It was one of several pieces of meteorite discovered - together they weighed 32 kilograms (about 70 pounds).
The Moon and Planet Institute describes EC 002 as "a relatively coarse-grained, brown and beige appearance with sporadic large green, yellow-green and less yellow-brown crystals".
The rough identification of EC002 classified it as an achondritis, which immediately distinguished it from most meteorites that are chondrites.
The chondrites are dogplanetary rocky rocks formed billions of years ago by the dust and mineral deposits of the early solar system.
The Achondrites are rocks torn off from the surface of planetary bodies, so they are newer.
Their composition can provide a key to solving the processes of forming their sources, as they demonstrate characteristics corresponding to the period of internal melting, separating the core from the bark of the young planet.
They are also relatively rare.
Of the tens of thousands of meteorites listed in the Meteroitical Bulletin database, only 3179 are anchondrites.
Most of the meteorites found, about 95%, come from two bodies in space, and about 75% can be derived from one large asteroid called 4 Vesta.
And the anchondrites are usually basalt, originating from the basalt crust of planetary bodies.
EC 002 - no.
It's a volcanic rock called andesite.
Scientists measured the radioactive isotopes of aluminum and magnesium that it contains, and concluded that the minerals crystallized about 4.565 billion years ago.
The Earth, on the contrary, is only 4.54 billion years old, writes BigThink.
This piece of cold magma is a lonely relic of a distant past.

On Earth, an ancient material was discovered that is several hundred million years older than the Solar System.
A recent study showed that the microscopic grains of dust originated in some distant star about 7 billion years ago.
When compared with the Sun, its age is only 4.6 billion years, reports the portal PLANETANOVOSTI.COM.
These tiny ancient grains came to the ground during the meteorite's fall.
Meteorites often contain such "presolar grains", but it is very difficult to find them, because they are deep inside the stone.
Presolar grains were contained in a huge meteorite, Merchison, whose weight exceeded 100 kilograms.
It exploded over Australia in the fall of 1969 and fell into a huge number of small pieces.
52 kilograms of meteorite were sent to the museum for further study.
As a result, it was possible to detect a lot of mineral grains, which experts call silicon carbide.
Thanks to this finding, scientists were able to learn more about how the stars are formed and what happens to them in the initial stages.
I can't believe how long these microscopic grains had to go before they hit Earth.

Machu Picchu is a city with polished stone walls built in accordance with the classic architecture of the Incas.
The first building of this complex is "Intihuatana", the Sun Temple, a room with three windows and an area of Machu Picchu, which archaeologists call the Holy Zone.
Machu Picchu was built around 1450 at the height of the Inca Empire.
The city was abandoned about 100 years later, following the victory of the Spanish Empire.
In 1913, the site became famous after the National Geographic Society had fully devoted its April discussions.
In 1981, an area of 32592 km ² surrounded by Machu Picchu was registered as a historical heritage of Peru.
The area was not limited to these ruins but also included the regional natural landscapes.
Machu Picchu was registered as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983, and the area was called "a pure masterpiece of architecture and a unique symbol of the Inca civilization."
This work is now known as one of the Seven New Wonders.

The authorities of Tenerife will prohibit tourists from building towers of stones Moscow.
July 30.
INTERFAX.RU - Tenerife authorities want to ban tourists from building stone towers on the beaches of the island, as they are harmful to the environment, the newspaper The Sun reports.
"Biologists claim that pebble pyramids violate the island's ecosystem.
The defenders of nature propose to install posters reminiscent of the consequences of the construction of stone sculptures, as well as to introduce a ban at the legislative level, "the publication writes.
As the newspaper notes, tourists flock to pebble beaches for photos.
However, local environmentalists oppose the construction of pyramids, as some arthropods use pebbles as a shelter.
"In addition, in the lower part of the stones grow tiny algae, which feed crustaceans, reptiles, molluscs.
When the pebbles are constantly moved, the volume of these algae decreases, "says the Sun biologist Pedro Luis Sánchez.
The tradition of building stone towers was formed after the meditation event "Harmonic Convergence", which took place in 1987.
People gathered in sacred places of the planet, such as the Heops pyramid in Egypt, Lake Baikal in Siberia, the Uluru rock in Australia, the city of Glastonbury in the UK, Machu Picchu in Peru, the Haleakala volcano in Hawaii and Mount Shasta in the United States.
"In the United States, the participants of the event collected sculptures from stones, and now tourists are trying to reproduce this on the beaches of Tenerife," the publication writes.
According to the director of the Telesforo Bravo Juan Coello Nature Conservation Foundation, Jaime Coello, tourists naively believe that pebbles towers are part of local culture associated with pagan beliefs.
"In Scotland or Peru, such sculptures can be considered as ancient burial places, but in Spain there was no such thing," the expert emphasizes.
As The Sun notes, now local volunteers and fund staff are forced to constantly clean up the beaches.

MOSCOW, July 15 – RIA Novosti.
Russian and foreign scientists working with the LHCb detector as part of the Large Hadron Collider have discovered hints of the existence of extremely rare and short-lived particles – Λb-barions, whose existence was predicted more than thirty years ago.
This is reported by the press service of the Institute of Nuclear Physics of the SB RAS.
"We know what quarks exist, what laws they obey, but unfortunately there is no mathematical apparatus that would reveal the richness of the world of particles.
Therefore, we are forced to use approximate methods to predict their properties, and often they contradict each other.
Therefore, every observation of the new state is a big event, "said Anton Poluektov of the National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS) of France.
The LHCb detector was created by CERN specifically to search for particles that do not fit into the Standard Model of Physics, too heavy to search for them directly.
The physicists working on it study the behavior of so-called B-mesons – unusual particles consisting of b-quark and some other elementary particle.
Over the past two years, several groups of scientists who have studied the traces of the breakup of the B-mesons have found many hints that this process can not always be described using the Standard Model of Physics.
So far, none of the open phenomena - anomalies in the disintegration of neutral strange B-mesons and their "charged" fellows, as well as the unequal frequency of disintegration of the taons and muons generated by them, have received the status of scientific discovery due to the insufficient amount of accumulated data.
"With the three quarks of which the barions are made up, theorists work harder, and there is almost no experimental data with adorable barions.
But there is a theoretical model, developed in 1986, even before the opening of the first adorable barion, which predicts two particles with very close masses, "said Ivan Belyaev of the Institute of Theoretical and Experimental Physics in Moscow.
Now BAC and all its devices are on a long "vacation", which will end only in early 2021, but this does not prevent physicists from analyzing the data already collected and looking for traces of new particles predicted by the Standard Model or going beyond it.
Last weekend Belyaev, Poluektov and their colleagues announced the discovery of two new states of the so-called Λb-barion - particles of two lungs and one "charming" quark.
They are as interesting to scientists in terms of finding traces of "new physics" as B-mesons, but their properties and theorists, and experimenters know even less than their smaller counterparts.
Analyzing the data collected by LHCb during the last session of the BAC, Russian and foreign physicists managed to discover at once two new states of Λb-barion, potentially associated with the existence of two different particles – Λb (1D) -barion and Σb-barion.
Their mass is 6146 and 6152 mega-electronvolts, which is slightly larger than that of a single adorable quark.
So far, as Belyaev noted, scientists can not say what they were able to detect, and he hopes that the resumption of observations and experiments on other particle accelerators will help to understand which of these two versions is closer to the truth.

The study is being prepared for publication in the Archaeological Review, and Phys.org.
It is a tomb located on the UNESCO World Heritage Site.
As part of a new study, archaeologists once again infiltrated the Meishau mound, which is an ancient tomb of corridor type.
They examined the side cameras in detail.
Previously, they were only known to have been buried.
However, a new analysis led scientists to an unexpected discovery.
The architecture of these side cameras was unusual.
Unlike the main hall of the tomb, they are literally built in a mirror reflection.
Scientists believe that the side cameras were "houses of the dead" and were designed to allow the buried without problems to enter the afterlife.
It is noteworthy that in general the layout of these cameras is similar to the layout of ordinary houses that existed in ancient times.
"Imagine that these stone walls would be wallpaper, and that if you had stuck the wallpaper upside down in some places, the patterns in their layout would be markedly different from each other," says the lead author of the work, Jay van der Reyden, from the University Institute of Archaeology. "The difference is the rearrangement of several architectural elements that were placed in ordinary houses on the right side, and here are placed on the left.
The side chambers are built to be inside the underworld, with the main walls acting as membranes, dividing this life and the next. "
Interestingly, the tomb of Meishau has been known to archaeologists for more than 150 years.
All this time, excavations were carried out here.
It would seem that a thoroughly studied object can no longer reveal new secrets.
However, the new study proves that this is not the case.
The discovery also raised new questions for researchers.
For example, what kind of society built this huge neolithic monument, what was its cult?
The practice of building "houses for the dead" was quite common in Europe.
But they weren't upside down.

The discovery of archaeologists is reported by the Mexican National Institute of Anthropology and History INAH.
Applying Lidar radar technology in field research in the municipality of Candelaria, Campeche, scientists have identified more than two thousand objects built by pre-Spanish civilizations.
First, the area was studied from the air, then experts used radar scans to detect anomalies under the ground.
After analyzing the data obtained, scientists determined the exact coordinates of archaeological sites, as well as their size and shape.
Field surveys were then carried out on the ground.
At a site of 223 km from Palenque to Escarsega, researchers registered 553 archaeological sites, many of which were previously unknown to scientists.
Particular attention of specialists attracted monumental complexes near the city of Candelaria.
One of them includes 91 pre-Spanish buildings, including ceremonial buildings and residential buildings hidden under several mounds.
Among them were found pyramidal bases, square and rectangular foundations and terraces.
The second complex comprises 37 buildings with monumental religious and residential functions.
In the second section, 234 km from Eskarseg to Kalkini, 1,634 archaeological sites were identified, including residential buildings, public and religious buildings.
Many of them were found for the first time.

Andreas Wagner might as well try an amazing experiment at home.
Put the wheat in the container and seal the mouth with your dirty coat for 21 days, and you will see the mouse.
Not only new mice, but also adults.
Don't be surprised, at least this is the description of Joan Baptista Van Helmont, a 17th-century physician and chemist.
(He also said that by placing Loller between the two bricks, a scorpion would appear in the sunlight.)
Van Helmand was not the first to make this hypothesis, and the spontaneous generation can be traced back at least to Aristotle, but Van Helmand was one of the last proponents.
Now, if scientists suggest that wheat and underwear work together to create a new life, it must be regarded as a lunatic never to turn over, but Van Helmand's hasty experiments did not cause much disturbance, and he was still a respected man when he passed away in 1644.
Nature is said to have been widely accepted by the public at that time, and his experiments only verified what was apparent.
After several decades of his life, Francesco Redi, an Italian physician, showed the world how similar experiments were done.
He placed the meat in a wide-mouth bottle, and after a long period of time, the bottle was filled with maggots.
However, this does not occur naturally, and if Reddy seals the bottle with cotton cloth, the flies will not be able to lay eggs in the flesh and will not produce maggots.
Riddy prompted natural occurrences to say accelerated decline, as did the 17th-century Dutch Antonie van Leeuwenhoek.
He is a cloth maker and a lens grinder who creates microscopes that open the way to the microbiological world.
For some time, microbes were much smaller than visible organisms, and thus provided a perfect word for natural occurrences, such as John Needham, a Scottish priest, who in the mid-18th century proposed the idea of corrupt organic organisms creating microbes.
A century later, the results of Louis Pasteur's experiments contradict Niedan's view that microbes cause the corruption of organic matter, contrary to Niedan's theory.
Pasteur's thorough sterilization of the nutrient-rich broth and the surrounding air showed that no new life had been created, and the experiment was a verdict on the death of nature.
Pasteur was able to prove that natural occurrences were incorrect, but he and his contemporaries did not understand the reason behind it, because the origin of life was not a biologist but a chemist ’ s problem. The problems experienced by 19th century chemists, like the Mendelists who tried to understand life ’ s innovation in the early 20th century – they were all born too early.
Dmitri Mendeleev barely completed the periodic table, but the chemistry of life remains blank.
It is true that fundamental chemistry, at the time of its cost, came out by virtue of its ability to become a venerable science, perhaps deeply rooted in alchemy.
In the 20th century, a Nobel laureate quantum physicist, Wolfgang Pauli, after his first wife and a chemist, said to friends, "I can understand if she runs away with a bullfighter, but it's just an ordinary chemist..."
A century later, we know that the enormous stumbling blocks that come naturally are the possibility or the impossibility of the complex manifestation of life.
Even a protein or a specific amino acid sequence is unlikely to occur naturally.
In the case of E. coli, it contains millions of proteins and other complex molecules and is unlikely to appear naturally.
Modern biochemistry allows us to estimate the possibility, and also overturns the notion that complex organisms originate from natural creation.
This does not mean that natural creation has not occurred in the early history of life, and the natural origin of life requires it, but occurs in simpler forms than modern cells or modern proteins.
The first form of life on Earth is more like a bull's car than a Ferrari, and more aptly, like a wheel than a bull's car.
Even the wheels are not created with great leaps and bounds, but with a lot of discreet steps.
The silt of time eroded the footprints of the first form of life, but chemists have reconstructed some steps.
Chemists have demonstrated not only how life happens, but also the crucial point: before life emerges, the creative power of nature has applied the same rules as it is today.
Both at the time and now, new chemical reactions and molecules are needed to renew a more progressive life.
The Hadean Eon, which marks the origin of Earth's geology for more than 4 billion years, is so subtly named after the Greek word Hell, because early Earth was like Hell, and the surface of liquid magma was covered in vaporized rock atmosphere.
Even after the surface has solidified into a solid crust, the earth is not a fascinating place.
If you look at the Earth like hell from outer space, you can see the surface of the earth is full of holes and dents, countless volcanoes are hot, hot rain is constantly pouring into the original ocean, only a lot of atmospheric pressure than today 's density can prevent the ocean from being boiled dry.
Needless to say, just one breath is enough to kill you, and the toxicity in the atmosphere comes from deadly amounts of carbon dioxide and hydrogen.
Finding shelter was a good idea, as many large asteroids during the late Heavy Bombardment hit early Earth.
Although most of these ancient signs of dramatic change disappear due to the constant movement of the surface, you can see the huge crater on the moon at night, leaving scars that can still make people shudder.
We have learned from the slow ticking of chemical clocks in ancient rocks that the age of the Earth, the decay of radioactive materials such as uranium, records ancient history.
When the worst period passed, it was most notable that life began at an alarming rate.
About 3.8 billion years ago, just a few billion years after the birth of the Earth, less than a tenth of today's history, the first microbial fossils appeared.
Even near the incredible demarcation line of 3.8 billion years ago, traces of antiquarian metabolites appeared in the rocks of Sigringland in the form of lighter isotopes of carbon.
Life does not waste any time and appears almost at a fast pace, which tells us that the origins of life and the creative power behind it may not be so difficult to happen, and that this innovative power may be as old as life itself.
The origins of early life on Earth clearly require a set of chemical theories.
One of the earliest is "primordial soup", usually attributed to Alexander Oparin and Hordan, who proposed the modern evolutionary synthesis, which they put forward in the 1920s.
It is worth noting, however, that Darwin had this idea half a century earlier than they were. In 1871, Darwin, in a letter to Joseph Dalton Hooker, speculated: "If (oh, how big if) we could think of adding a variety of ammonia phosphates, light, heat, electricity and so on to some warm ponds." By chemical reaction, protein compounds are formed and are prepared for more complex changes.
At the same time, Darwin gives us a great reason why today we may be disappointed to gaze at such a warm little pond: the material in the pond may be "swallowed or absorbed" by today's creatures.
For decades, the original soup was only speculative, until 1952, when Harold Urey, a Nobel laureate, helped Stanley Miller, a graduate student at the University of Chicago Laboratory.
Based on projections of the gas composition in the early atmosphere, Miller sealed the gas in a container, simulating the initial lightning shock with a large amount of spark, and simulating the rainfall flush mixture with a large amount of condensate.
A few days later, many organic molecules, usually created by organisms, appear in Miller's miniature world.
This is a historical discovery of how organic molecules were produced from inorganic matter in the early days of the turbulent Earth.
Miller ’ s primordial oceans not only produce organic molecules, but also create the basic building blocks of modern proteins, such as glycine and alanine. Subsequent experiments have continued to produce many other raw materials for life, including sugar and parts of DNA.
More importantly, Miller's experiment shifted the origins of life from philosophical conjecture to a difficult, evidence-based science.
In September 1969, the world learned what Miller did not know in 1952: that life molecules were able to appear in an environment that was more unfavorable than the early days of the Earth.
That September, about 160 km north of Melbourne, Australia, and a few hundred people in the small town of Murchison, an explosive meteor briefly created the second Sun in the sky. The meteorite ruptured left a towing dust and smaller debris, the largest of which fell in the barn, fortunately not causing disaster.
The cosmic accident occurred two months after the first human walk to the Moon, when scientists were keen to study meteorites from outer space.
After the heat ebbed, scientists discovered that the Mercheson meteorite transported a very unusual cargo. The meteorite, as ancient as Earth, has been traveling in outer space for hundreds of millions of years, carrying several kinds of amino acids that make up protein, as well as important components of DNA — purine and pyrimidine.
Subsequent studies using spectroscopy in the 21st century have confirmed that meteorites contain more than 10,000 different organic molecules, although many of these components are extremely rare.
It is worth noting that the Mercheson meteorite is not a malevolent act of nature. The events of the fall of the meteorite into the Earth are endless, with countless types of rocks penetrating the sky and transporting organic matter to Earth.
Fortunately, we do not have to wait for another meteorite to fall, because molecules in the universe show their own structure by absorbing or emitting radiation, and through the highly sensitive ears of radio telescopes, hundreds of different organic molecules in the interstellar cloud can be heard whispering to us.
In fact, these molecules are screaming because three quarters of the interstellar cloud are organic molecules that contain key ingredients of life, such as glylamine acid.
By the way, the most abundant triatomic molecule in the interstellar cloud is water, which is a declaration of how unusual we and our planet are.
In the universe, the simplest composition of life is everywhere, and molecules from outer space may sow the seeds of life on Earth. Meteorites and comets (especially those that hit the Earth early) release more than 10 times the total amount of water and more than a thousand times the current atmosphere.
More importantly, they also provide a wealth of living resources, that is, the amount of organic molecules we find in interstellar space is incredible, at least 10 megatons of spectra, perhaps hundreds of times higher than that amount of organic carbon from outer space into our atmosphere, at least 10 times more than the carbon content in living cells circulating today.
The dust towing behind the comet is no less important.
These dust passes through the Earth's orbit, unlike the white heat and high temperatures caused by giant meteorites during the fall, which destroy some organic molecules, the dust of comets in order to see the invisible and continuous rain of life seeds covering the Earth.
Maybe we really are made up of stardust.
We may never know whether most life molecules are created in outer space or on Earth, but in any case, these discoveries contain a few simple and important questions: first, life molecules appear naturally in the right environment; second, the environment does not need to be a warm pond as Darwin says, or a very special place in the universe, which can be beyond a few light years, or just like the interstellar atmosphere. Not at all.
The third is the subject of innovation, which I have already mentioned, and which still stands today: innovation revolves around new molecules and reactions to their creation.
To understand creativity, we must understand the origin of these molecules.
Book Description This article is an extract from How Life is Innovative: Where is Nature ’ s Evolution and Innovation?, published by Marco Polo * through the link above, the Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical Critical.
Author: Andreas Wagner Translator: More than a hundred years after Gianjong Darwin published The Origin of Species, there is always a difficult question: How does "innovation" occur in nature?
In 1859, Darwin published The Origin of Species, and his theory of natural selection explains how useful adaptability can be preserved over time, but also creates the biggest question of evolution: "How does the adaptability of living things occur?"
Is it true that the random mutations that have occurred over 3.8 billion years since the birth of the first life on Earth can be responsible for wings, eyeballs, camouflage, lactose digestion, photosynthesis, and other surprises created by nature?
If the answer is no, then what is the speed of evolution and the mechanism behind relative efficiency?
When Darwin didn ’ t know what mutations, genes, DNA double helix, transcription and translation, DNA sequencing, genetic, biochemistry, molecular biology, and even processing a great deal of information, he suggested 160 years ago that he had opened up a hundred interesting discoveries in biology — now, we have the ability to make a huge mystery of “biological adaptability. ” The regiment is approaching!
The world-renowned evolutionary biologist Andreas Wagner, with more than 15 years of research, presents the puzzle of the loss of Darwinian theory.
Using the unexpected experimental and computational techniques of early scientists, he found that it was not only by accident, but a set of rules that allowed nature to discover new molecules and operating mechanisms at a fraction of the cost of random variation.
With meticulous research, careful testimony, charismatic writing, and a list of fascinating examples, How Life Is Innovative presents the last piece of the puzzle of life ’ s rich and varied nature.

Chen Bingyang, a PhD student in Oriental Art History at the University of Bonn in Germany, has been a researcher and curator of the National Museum of Art in Beijing in recent years. He is a young scholar in the Himalayan art circle.
Chen originally studied law at the University of Taiwan and spent some time in finance after graduation.
My love for Himalayan art, or for Himalayan and South Asian culture, should originate from my interest in Tibetan and Indian cultural geography, which included several national geography magazines, an encyclopedia, and some Western explorers of the 19th and 20th centuries. The possibility of study began after his travels to Tibet, India, Nepal, and Southeast Asia.
"Art history can be combined with history, geography, cultural studies, anthropology and other subjects that I was interested in, which is the main reason why I chose to invest in art history research.
It was not easy for me to start on this path and try to combine my interests, but now I can say that research in the history of art has brought me a great deal of happiness. "
From the border point of view of the core in 2014, Chen Bingyang traveled to Nepal ’ s Anapurna Mountain District, where he was destined for the Muthtang area.
Remembering that journey, he said with excitement, "The Muthtang area is a very special place in Nepal! Perhaps we can call it a Tibetan cultural enclave under the cultural sphere of contemporary Nepal, which is dominated by Indian and South Asian culture.
It is located on the border between Himalayas Annapuna and the northern slope of the Dalagiri Mountains and Tibet, and is separated from the rest of Nepal.
From today's point of view, it may be a remote and remote place, but it has existed for a long time in the form of an independent kingdom, with Tibetan Buddhist Sakha as its cultural core, where the people, language, culture and so on are closer to Tibet in the north. "
When Chen Bingyang crosses the Kaligandaki valley and runs up the Himalayas to the upper reaches of the Muthang, beside him are the seventh peak of the world, Dalagiri, and the tenth peak, Anapuna.
"In the past millennium, Muthtang has also been an important traffic channel for communication between South Asia and the Tibetan Plateau, and the footpath I have taken has left countless business trips, practitioners, monks and pilgrims in the past.
"I was very lucky to have met Dr. Christian Luczanits, a famous Himalayan art historian, and had a chance to learn from him. Cultural Heritage Site.
Therefore, I have always felt that this may be some kind of coincidence. "
Chen went on to say that the West Himalayan region (mainly the present-day Ali Prand region in Tibet and the Spiti Valley, Ladakh and Zangka in India).
Muthang also belongs to the western Himalayan region, which has always been a place of cultural exchange in the region, and because of its geographical independence, various cultural factors have been able to produce a culture with local characteristics after the meeting, and have the dual characteristics of "cultural border - cultural center".
"In the 15th century, Himalayan art was the key to the development of Himalayan art, and various regional styles rose on the snowy plateau.
Works that appear in this era allow you to look back at past artistic developments and often hint at future trends.
In the Greater West Himalayan region of the time, the Kingdom of Mushtang and the Kingdom of Gurgle (sometimes referred to as the Kingdom of the New Gurgle in Western scholarly writings as distinct from the Kingdom of Pran-Gurgle, which flourished in the late 10th to early 12th century).
The two kingdoms were at that time the main political actors and patrons of art in the West Himalayas, and their artistic performances reflected their cultural and social features.
The decision of the visual viewer of art is often a subjective factor, and we have been able to guess through images what happened to the people under it, which has been the most interesting thing to study art. It should be extended to focus on the relationship between art and its political and cultural background in the 15th-century Kingdom of Mustang.
How to define “Himalayan Art ”?
Muktinath is also a sacred place for Buddhism and Hinduism.
According to Himalayan art pioneer Jeff Watt, "Himalayan style art" is derived from the Himalayas, mainly from Tibet, Nepal, Kashmiri, Bhutan, India and northern Pakistan, and other regions around which these cultures radiate.
The surrounding areas where Himalayan "style" art works can be found include China, Mongolia, southern Siberia, Tuva, the Buryat Republic, and northern Myanmar.
In China, for example, there is a Himalaya-style Buddha tower in Beijing, and there are stone carvings under the peak of Hangzhou, which can be seen in Himalayas art that has passed down from the Yuan Dynasty.
It is evident that Himalayan art has a long history of influence and a wide area of radiation.
However, the development of Himalayan art and its research system from the middle of the last century is still only in its initial stage, compared to the history of art in other eras or regions. Chen says, "The so-called Himalayan art history, or academic discipline, is still a relatively new concept. It has only been developed over the past two decades from South Asian art to East Asian art history, Tibetan studies and religious studies. It's coming out.
It can be seen that in a situation of limited objects and difficult field work, there is still a lot of work to be done in the study of the history of Himalayan art, including field study and recording of more local works to establish a more complete database, the integration of technical linguistics, the establishment of the relationship between art and text, and so on. Wait, wait.
“As the knowledge system of the history of Himalayan art is complex and the academic system is still incomplete, it may be difficult for some beginners to study, but it also means that there is a lot of room for this subject to develop. Interested people will find that there are many different faces to explore! ”
Chen further commented on the differences between East and West scholars in the study of Himalayan art, "Today, Western interest in Himalayan art stems primarily from the study of Eastern philosophy during the 19th century, and their subsequent fascination with Eastern religious philosophy and culture in the 1960s and 1970s."
Many Western collectors and Himalayan art scholars are also often explorers, travelers, or geologists.
This unique feature shapes the Western Himalayan academic and collection circles.
This trend is reflected in Western collections and research, which can be broadly divided into studies of tanka and figurines trotting into Western collections, as well as local studies of monastic murals in the West Himalayan region, Nepal, Bhutan, and so on.
In the collection, Western Tibetans may be more inclined to work from the 15th century onwards.
At the same time, researchers included specialists in research institutes, as well as many independent scholars of traveler, explorer and collector origin. "
"In the east, Himalayan art research is mostly conducted in specialized research institutions, and due to its geographical advantages, it has been possible to study the site mural of sites including Kangzao, Ando, Weizang and Ali.
Several major research units today focus on field research.
In addition, the Qing Palace collection of the National Palace Museum in Beijing and the collection of new collectors in the past 20 years are also important research objects.
Traditionally, East Asian Tibetans and research are generally considered to prefer the collection of late Ming and Qing court Himalayas, while Western Tibetans and their research prefer early works from Nepal, Tibet and Tibet, a view that has cultural and research traditions, but has gradually changed in recent years. "
It is interesting to note that the Zhi Guan Art Museum, located at Xianliangji Temple in Beijing, is a private collection of Himalayan art in China. Unlike Chinese collectors who generally love the Ming and Qing courts, it is a large collection of early images of South Asia and Tibet.
In 2018, Chen Bingyang, who joined the research and curatorial work, came into contact with the preparatory stage of the "Buddha's Light," the exhibition of Buddhist statues of the National Palace Museum and the Zhoguan Museum of Art, which will be opened at the Palace Palace Museum at that time.
The exhibition features 112 exhibits from the National Palace Museum of Beijing, the National Museum of Art, and some private collections. The exhibition is based on bronze statues from ancient northwest India, central India, northeastern India, Nepal and Tibet.
"At that time, the National Palace Museum accounted for about one-third of the museum's exhibits, and their Ming and Qing court statues and ancient Buddhist statues of South Asia were complementary to each other.
It can be said that the joint exhibition with the National Palace Museum is not only a major milestone since the establishment of the National Museum of Fine Arts, but also a unique opportunity for the exhibition itself to bring together a number of outstanding pieces and introduce the source of Tibetan Buddhist art and its rich artistic features. "
In addition, the internationalization of the exhibition is one of the highlights of the exhibition, which also contributed to the special exhibition from the Zhiguan Museum of Art, which was launched last year at the Rubin Museum of Art in New York. In the 15th century, there was a great deal of tension between the Tibetan people and the Tibetan people.
“The main part of my work is probably the best part, which is to keep in touch with scholars from all over the world. In addition to intellectual learning, I am often encouraged by their dedication and passion for art research. ”
The education and promotion of Himalayan art is a direction that needs to be taken seriously in the future in the Kaligandaki Valley of Nepal.
This river shaped the natural and cultural landscape of the Musiang and gave birth to the unique culture and art of the Musiang.
Since most of the Himalayan art is religious art, it is difficult to understand the true meaning and development of Himalayan art without first understanding its religious history and theory, but such a threshold is not easy for most art lovers. In fact, Himalayan art education is also a part of Mr. Watt 's strong desire to promote improvement.
Mr. Watt has a high position in the field of Himalayan art research, and I am honored and grateful to have the opportunity to work with him and to participate in the Himalayan Art Resources (HAR) that he founded.
This website collects a wealth of Himalayan art material and related knowledge, and most importantly, Mr. Watt advocates explaining the concept of Himalayan art in plain and easy language.
Recently, he has also started to launch a series of videos on HAR ’ s Instagram account. Anyone who has questions about Himalayan art can leave a message to ask him, and he will record a short video to answer questions. "
Tabo Monastery, located in the Spiti Valley, preserves stunning and deeply artistic wall paintings and clay sculptures.
Mr Chan lamented that the time he spent with Mr Watt had benefited greatly, "I am particularly impressed by his passion for graphic knowledge and educational promotion of Himalayan art.
In contrast to the academic and curator, Mr Watt emphasized that he was an educator.
I think that no matter what the subject is, only the highest level of experts will be able to introduce such a wide range of content in a simple and interesting way after integration.
Chen Ping-yang laughs that he is only a Himalayan art beginner, and is now focusing on learning and academic research.

In Taiwan, where the Confucian culture in East Asia is concerned, the patriarchal family culture of the Han people has a profound influence on the values of the past.
Thus, especially after the lifting of martial law, women in Taiwan pursue subjectivity, with their cultural, physical, and psychological context and needs.
The development of the women's rights movement has an important historical context in Taiwan society and has become an important part of the social movement in Taiwan.
However, even today, the problem of the upbringing of women in society and in the workplace still exists.
YANG Yuen-ying quotes Lynne Tirrell's research into the plight of women in politics who are often attacked by "girls".
In discussing the sexual function of language, she noted that calling a mature woman a girl is tantamount to arranging a specific role function, denying her adult status and rationalizing paternalism for men.
The term "girl" not only rationalizes her salary, distrusts her in making major decisions, but also denies her autonomy and ability to judge behavior.
As soon as it is given a weak position, the relative inferior treatment comes with it.
The recent outbreak of the epidemic in Taiwan has made society more nervous and filled with some blame witch-hunting fears.
We know that even though we have always been proud to think that Taiwanese society is already very diverse and inclusive in terms of gender issues, some witchcraft talk will emerge in times of crisis, especially some unprovoked attacks against female politicians.
We may as well rethink the role of female politicians in today ’ s era by using a dialogue novel, “The Rainbow Ribbon ”, with different strokes from the author.
In particular, many of these stories may offer some consolation at a time when the epidemic is difficult and critical.
With the key of mysterious figures, the book opens the inner chamber of the most moving women to see, using nine famous women in the world × nine rainbow figures × nine world famous paintings to tell a series of interesting and touching stories.
The book depicts Reiko Iwasaki, who lives in Kyoto, as a descendant of Japan ’ s famous financial valve. In her daily life with her husband, Kenzo Fujiwara, and son Fujiwara, she uses her inherited family ’ s autobiography, “Rainbow Life Mathematics, ” to illustrate the life challenges faced by women with her keen observation and empathy.
These include: Hilary, who failed to run for president, Hiroshi Abe of Japan, Prime Minister Mekel of Germany, and Park Geun-hye before she fell into the door of her best friend.
Sandberg, singer Angelina Jolie, who ended a much-admired marriage, was also impeached by former President Rousseff of Brazil, and American talk-show host Oprah.
Each is the most internationally recognized female leader of the day.
The author of the book combines characters and fiction in the real world, integrating readers into the eyes of several famous women, bringing out their vivid living experiences in the face of the challenges of their lives, singing their success and lamenting their loss.
Through the dialogue between the three protagonists, we see the beauty and sadness of these women in their bright and beautiful appearance, and the courage not to bow to fate in all kinds of adversity.
For example, the analysis of the fate of Rousseff, the first woman president in Brazilian history who has been impeached and who is unfamiliar with the Taiwanese, is rather playful: It is conceivable that a woman like Rousseff would be able to shake her head all the way in a large state business, and to control the dragon-headed throne that monopolizes the state's energy supply. In addition, it is necessary to operate the resources of state-owned enterprises, as a private political capital, to be lost to the political activities of their political parties and become their own political investment.
The long term exchange of interests has accumulated, and it has allowed her to climb to the peak of the head of state.
Such skills and achievements are definitely not what the mediocre generation can achieve.
In my opinion, on the other side of the gold trading, Rousseff should also have the character of being generous and caring for the weak.
In contrast to the stereotype of politicians, Reiko always analyzes every real person from a more human point of view.
The above statement, which appears to be a reinvention of the political image of this female political elite in Brazil, is clearly not only aimed at this particular Brazilian object, but also widely described and introspected, all good female politicians in the necessary sphere of politics in the exchange of good and evil choices and the battle of the natural man.
These contents have a profound meaning for East Asian society, which also has a deep patriarchal ideology and likes to demonize and nurture female elites.
The author Fujiwara Kenzo, who has lived in Japan for a long time, describes Kyoto ’ s food, travel, art, and current affairs, and has a unique experience.
However, Fujiwara is actually only the author's name, and even though he has all sorts of things like his own, he is not really a Japanese, but rather a Taiwanese who works in Chinese and is also an outstanding political figure who has dedicated himself to the administration of local government in the past.
He was praised as "King Maker" by President Chen, the son of Taiwan, and was a real proponent of President Chen's introduction to later President Tsai Ing-wen.
However, as a result of the controversy and betrayal brought about by the political struggle between political opponents and teammates, an outstanding figure who was once a young man was put under arrest for a moment and turned into a corrupt criminal who was even criticized by the rank and file.
This was the first time that he was in prison.
At that time, he knelt in prison and squatted to write, he said, "Lying on a sheet of paper on the thigh, using the time of light in the room, taking a breath, writing for 8 to 12 hours. After writing, it is always painful to the back of the waist, it is empty and satisfied.
This derivative, which has long been fixed in a single position, is that the paper, which relies on the knee of the point and the buttocks of the body's center of gravity, creates a thick layer of thick cocoon in friction.
In addition to focusing on these female elites, this work has an important highlight.
The authors often sacrifice love for all human beings and seek justice in the world, but the great sages who suffer from it make their grievances and rattle unjustly: There are many apostles who have true knowledge and faith, but these few believers are often unwilling to compromise with the mainstream religious organizations and suffer from the irreverence of the so-called Orthodox Church. The Rev. Cheng Ming-chien, who suppressed persecution and founded the Evangelical Church of Christianity, is a sad example.
A preacher like Reverend Cheng, who claims to listen to the Revelation in person and preach the resurrection of the Lord in the present world, is regarded as a heresy by the orthodox sect, and is imprisoned by the judiciary for a very absurd crime.
On the other hand, a man who worked as a police officer during the Japanese rule and worked as a monk, who had never been qualified as a priest but who called himself a pastor, and who had created a cult, and his father and daughter, committed a false political disorder.
What kind of political society is this? What kind of religious culture is this?
These sounds, which appear to be wretched and demanding justice for the great victims, show on the one hand the empathy of the author of the book, and on the other, the air of the author's benevolence.
In fact, it is also the author who hints at his own painful and lonely state of mind.
As noted critic Yang Chao once wrote in a book about Li Ao's book, "Every character in the novel, from Kang Youwei, Tam Si-tung to Liang Kai-chao, actually has a part of Li Ao's self-projection."
With the cover of the characters, Li Ao finally exposed the feelings and frustrations that were rarely seen in his other works.
Sentiment of the loneliness and frustration of the fire of the revolution, whether by blood or by tears in the price of life, the enlightenment of the group is long gone.
The stories described by writers, many and few, also tell the anguish of being subjected to the cruelty and ruthlessness of the real, mass masses.
Finally, the author expresses an endless sense of ingratitude and affection for the book's protagonist, Reiko (who in fact describes the author's own wife), in addition to maintaining family planning after her husband's imprisonment, and raising a son with mental disabilities.
Every Tuesday afternoon, the short meeting ended, the silence of the voice, the moment the iron door fell down, the sadness came out like a knife and cut, the helplessness of thinking, what can I do for her? After being deprived of everything, the only thing can be done, only written.
The story's protagonist, Reiko, is so bright in her life, but in real time, her situation is so dark.
The number one woman in our world has to bear the financial burden that I have left behind. She has to be busy teaching at university, consulting teaching on Rainbow numbers, and writing on art history; most of all, she has to be kept awake for 24 hours to care for a son who has been diagnosed by three psychiatrists as a four-obstacle type.
Thinking that every Tuesday afternoon, only three or four hours of sleep a day, she had to bite her arm and not drive in the long road tunnel, because she dozed off and hit the front car.
Thinking about it, the image and story of the figures of women began to emerge..................
These internal monologues reveal the writing of the book, and show that this light and genuine love between troubled couples is also an irreplaceable highlight of the book, which is moving.
In addition, this leapfrogging imagination of literature makes one wonder about the study of cultural memory by German scholar Jan Assmann.
Jan Asman believes that in the reconstructed images of the Bible, the history of religion is shown in the form of a crisis of remembrance, which is to a certain extent successful.
The Jewish people were able to hold on to their hopes: "The slave now will be a free man next year, and the host will surely return to Jerusalem tomorrow."
Jan Asman believes that a memory art has been developed since Deuteronomy, which is based on the separation of identity and territory.
The Jewish people built up linked memories on the basis of a highly complex memory, all of which have a counterfactual nature.
During the epidemic, when we put this book back into the author ’ s literary world, we can say that it provides a kind of counterfactual hope, in addition to the sobering, daily worrying and accurate figures in front of the television screen.

Machu Picchu, also known as one of the most famous tourist destinations and historical areas, has faced a waste accumulation crisis.
The future of Machu Picchu is facing a serious threat due to the waste left by tourists, according to ISNA and quoted by Anshnt Orijins.
This historic site, also registered on the UNESCO World Heritage List, is located in the southern part of Peru.
Machu Picchu attracts 1.6 million tourists from around the world every year, while Peruvian authorities have narrowed the visit to the site in recent years.
However, the popularity of this historic site has led Machu Picchu to face a serious waste crisis.
Tourists often leave their waste in this place, and this is the main reason for the new crisis of Machu Picchu.
According to the information published by the Upfront News website, tourists drop more than 5 tons of waste per day in the area, a large part of which includes plastic water bottles that eventually end up in the local river.
The waste accumulation deteriorated so much that UNESCO threatened to remove Machu Picchu from the World Heritage List two years ago.
This means that Machu Picchu is now facing serious concerns about being removed from the UNESCO World Heritage List, which would be a shame for the Peruvian authorities if it happened.
Rare plants in the area have also suffered serious injuries following the accumulation of waste left by tourists.
This problem has caused serious concern for local authorities, as it can also affect the number of tourists in the region who are critical to economic growth.
On the other hand, officials want to protect the heritage and the environment of the region.
In this regard, a private campaign has begun to recycle countless plastic bottles abandoned by tourists.
About one ton of these plastic bottles are recycled daily.
Urban laws prohibit the construction of large recycling centers, which is why now this private small recycling center faces a large volume of waste.
According to reports by Al Jazeera, the local authorities of Machu Picchu are trying to make the region the first environmentally friendly region in the country.
They want tourists to take their recyclable water bottles with them while visiting this historic place and leave the area.
Although the threat of the withdrawal of Machu Picchu from the UNESCO World Heritage List is not immediate, there is still a risk.
Earlier, the multibillion-dollar construction plan of the airport, which has been offered to shorten the time of travel for tourists to the area, received many criticisms.
Protesters believe that the airport construction program in the region is very short-sighted and will jeopardize this historic location in the long run.
Machu Picchu, also known as the Lost City of Inca, was built around 1450 at the height of the Inca Empire.
It is located 50 miles from the capital of the Inca, which is why it has never been found and destroyed like other Inca cities.
Over the centuries, the forests around the complex have grown and covered it, and for this reason it has remained hidden from sight and only a few knew of its existence.
On July 24, 1911, Machu Picchu was introduced to the Western world by American historian Hiram Bingham in a speech at Yale University.
He was guided by local guidance where he occasionally visited the site.
Machu Picchu was registered as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983, and as a pure masterpiece of architecture and a unique symbol of the Inca civilization is now known as one of the seven new wonders.
The end of the message

Masonry in Machu Picchu.

What stand standing standing from the waters of the Gulf of Finland encircling Kronstadt forts-citadel, played their protective role both in the war with the Swedes in the Petrovsky times, and during the Patriotic war!
Dock pool in Kronstadt.
100 m wide, 200 m long.

The construction, which was completed without Peter, only in 1952, stretched for 2.5 km.
But there were up to 10 ships.

What did Peter look at there then?
So, the water went out of the docks in 24 hours through a deep, peasant ravine, into the dock pool, and then into the bypass canal.
Side wall of the pool.

Its walls are laid with granite blocks, which, although dilapidated, are still impressive with their masonry.
There's a lot of granite in Kronstadt.

But then there is no mention of any granite processing plant in Kronstadt.
And technology at the beginning of the 18th century was not yet modern.
Unique polygonal granite masonry.

I managed to get to this remote corner of the pool, which is mostly hidden from human eyes.

But it is this angle that is unique to its amazing wall.

But there's a high probability that she's going deeper into the ground.
The photo shows that there are several types of masonry.

The point with the arrow marked the path, which, if anyone wants to go to Kronstadt to approach the hidden wall.

A picture of the masonry near.
Drawing of granite blocks.

What else is she interested in?

"I can ’ t say I don ’ t feel my feelings," says Chen Ju, who says that a pluralistic democratic society is something she has spent her entire life trying to pursue, and has also been criticized by multiple voices. Chen Ju said that how the future will make everyone feel confident and hopeful about her and all the Sixth Commission, depending on what we do in the future, "I will feel that this part of us will continue to examine us in the future."
In addition, Chen Ju said in her speech that this will be the final stage of her life, and she further explained that this is the final task of government.
She said, "I am from a very young age of 19 with the participation of the Taiwan Democratic Movement, from martial law to martial law in Taiwan, I am from an outside social movement to a public office in the system, and I can return to human rights work today. This is my last job."
